record_office_terminology.probate: County Clerk (wills, estate settlements, inventories); Fiduciary Supervisor
record_office_terminology.marriage_licenses: County Clerk (marriage licenses from county formation)
record_office_terminology.vital_records_state_start: 1917 (statewide birth/death registration; some county records earlier; delayed birth certificates common)

burned_county_authority: WV Archives and History Library, Charleston; Library of Virginia Lost Records Localities (for pre-1863 Virginia records); FamilySearch Research Wiki -- West Virginia Burned Counties; Kyle P. Smith, 'Guide to West Virginia County Records on Microfilm'
administrative_notes: West Virginia separated from Virginia in 1863 during the Civil War, making it the only state formed by seceding from a Confederate state. It was admitted as the 35th state on June 20, 1863. The state has 55 counties. Pre-1863 records were created under Virginia jurisdiction and many originals remain at the Library of Virginia (LVA) in Richmond. The WV Archives and History Library in Charleston (part of the WV Division of Culture and History) is the primary state repository, holding microfilmed county records, state government records, and vital records. West Virginia was a border state that had slavery until the state constitution abolished it in 1865. The state's economy has been shaped by salt production (early 1800s), timber (late 1800s), coal mining (1880s-present), and chemical manufacturing. The Mine Wars period (1912-1921) produced significant labor records. Two of WV's counties (Ohio and Monongalia) were formed in 1776 from the District of West Augusta, predating the state by 87 years.

core_repositories.source_name: WV Archives and History Library (Division of Culture and History)
core_repositories.repository: WV Archives and History Library, Charleston
core_repositories.description: West Virginia's primary state archives. Houses microfilmed county records (deeds, marriages, wills, court orders, tax lists), state government records, vital records (births and deaths from 1917), military records, newspapers, maps, and photographs. The Archives holds the most comprehensive collection of WV county records on microfilm. Research room in the Culture Center, Charleston.

core_repositories.source_name: Library of Virginia (LVA) -- Pre-1863 Virginia Records
core_repositories.description: Essential for pre-1863 research. WV counties were Virginia counties before statehood, and many original records remain at LVA including deed books, will books, court orders, marriage bonds, tax lists, and land patents. LVA's Chancery Records Index includes pre-1863 WV county cases. Virginia Land Office patents and grants cover WV territory.

core_repositories.source_name: WVU West Virginia and Regional History Center
core_repositories.description: Premier manuscript repository for West Virginia. Extensive collections of family papers, business records (coal, timber, glass, chemical companies), political papers, photographs, oral histories, and county-level materials. Mine Wars collections, labor history, and Appalachian studies resources. David and Nancy Stewart Collection and other major genealogical holdings.

Strong collections for southwestern West Virginia, particularly Cabell, Wayne, Lincoln, and surrounding counties. Appalachian oral history collections, coal industry records, Ohio River valley materials, and local family papers. Rosanna Blake Collection of Confederate States of America materials.

familysearch_collections.description: Indexed and browsable collections: county records (deeds, marriages, wills, court orders), vital records, cemetery records, and military records. Extensive microfilm partnership with WV Archives and LVA provides digitized county records. Also check Virginia collections for pre-1863 records.
Comprehensive research guide with county-specific pages, record availability, burned county information, and links to digitized microfilm. Includes guidance on pre-1863 Virginia record research.

newspapers_and_periodicals.description: Digitized historic West Virginia newspapers from the 1850s forward. Includes Wheeling Intelligencer, Charleston Gazette, and county weeklies. Pre-1863 newspapers listed under Virginia.

newspapers_and_periodicals.repository: WV Archives and History Library, Charleston
newspapers_and_periodicals.description: Microfilmed West Virginia newspapers held at the WV Archives. County weeklies and city dailies documenting legal notices, estates, community events, and obituaries. Essential for local history research.

enslaved_persons_and_african_american.source_name: WV Archives -- Enslaved Persons and African American Records
enslaved_persons_and_african_american.repository: WV Archives and History Library, Charleston / LVA
enslaved_persons_and_african_american.description: West Virginia was a border state with slavery until 1865. Personal property tax lists (naming enslaved by slaveholder), estate inventories, bills of sale, and free negro records at WV Archives and LVA (for pre-1863 records). The Kanawha Salines area had significant enslaved labor in salt production. Freedmen's Bureau records for WV at NARA and FamilySearch.

Records of the Bureau of Refugees, Freedmen, and Abandoned Lands for West Virginia (1865-1872). Labor contracts, marriage registrations, school reports, and complaint files. WV had a smaller but significant Freedmen's Bureau presence.

native_american_and_removal_records.source_name: Native American Records -- WV Archives and NARA
native_american_and_removal_records.repository: WV Archives and History Library / NARA
native_american_and_removal_records.description: Pre-contact and colonial-era records of Native peoples in present-day WV including Shawnee, Cherokee, Mingo (Seneca), and Delaware (Lenape). Frontier fort records, Lord Dunmore's War (1774) records, and colonial land patents on former tribal lands. Grave Creek Mound (Adena culture) archaeological records. Point Pleasant battle records. Most Native peoples were displaced by the late 18th century.

military_records.repository: WV Archives and History Library, Charleston
military_records.description: Civil War records are particularly important -- WV men served in both Union and Confederate armies. Union service records, Confederate pension applications (for WV residents who served the Confederacy), militia records, Revolutionary War records (under Virginia), War of 1812, and WWI/WWII draft cards. WV Adjutant General records. National Cemetery records at Grafton and Wheeling.

military_records.source_name: Civil War Soldiers and Sailors System -- West Virginia
military_records.description: Searchable database of Civil War service records for WV soldiers on both sides. WV contributed approximately 32,000 Union soldiers and an estimated 7,000-9,000 served in Confederate units. Cross-reference with Virginia Confederate records at LVA.

land_and_property.source_name: Virginia Land Office Patents and Grants (pre-1863)
land_and_property.description: Colonial and state land patents for present-day WV territory from the 1750s to 1863. Virginia used the headright system and later treasury warrants. Northern Neck Proprietary (Fairfax Grant) covered parts of the Eastern Panhandle (Berkeley, Jefferson, Morgan, Hampshire, Hardy, Mineral, Grant, Pendleton counties). Essential for pre-statehood land research.
Post-1863 state land grants, forfeited and delinquent land records, and land books. WV State Auditor maintains land tax records. County clerks hold deed books from county formation forward.

vital_records.repository: WV Department of Health and Human Resources, Charleston
vital_records.description: State vital records: births and deaths from 1917 (statewide registration). Some county records exist earlier. Marriage records kept at county level from county formation. Delayed birth certificates are common for pre-1917 births. Historical records transferred to WV Archives over time. Pre-1863 vital events must be found in county records, church records, or newspapers.

church_and_cemetery.source_name: WV Church Records -- WV Archives and Local Congregations
church_and_cemetery.repository: WV Archives and History Library, Charleston / Local congregations
church_and_cemetery.description: Church records are especially important in WV for periods before civil registration. Baptist, Methodist, Presbyterian, Lutheran, Reformed, and Brethren congregations predominate. German churches in the Eastern Panhandle and Potomac Highlands. Coal mining communities had diverse congregations including Catholic, Orthodox, and Jewish. WV Archives has some microfilmed church records.

church_and_cemetery.source_name: Find A Grave -- West Virginia Cemeteries
church_and_cemetery.description: Crowdsourced cemetery transcriptions for West Virginia. Coverage varies by county. Family cemeteries on hillsides and hollows are common in WV and may not be in Find A Grave. WV Archives has cemetery survey records. WVGenWeb cemetery project has additional transcriptions.

research_guides_and_societies.description: Statewide historical society associated with the WV Division of Culture and History. Publishes West Virginia History journal. Maintains research resources and sponsors programs.
Volunteer-contributed county-level genealogy resources including cemetery transcriptions, Bible records, county histories, and research guides for all 55 West Virginia counties.

historical_counties_extinct.description: West Virginia has no extinct counties. All 55 counties that existed at or after statehood (1863) continue to exist. However, researchers must understand that all WV counties were Virginia counties before 1863. Counties formed before 1863 have parent counties that may be in present-day Virginia (e.g., Mercer was formed from Giles and Tazewell, both now in Virginia; Berkeley was formed from Frederick, now in Virginia). The District of West Augusta (1776-1780) was an informal Virginia jurisdiction that encompassed much of present-day WV before formal county organization. Monongalia and Ohio counties were the first formed from this district in 1776.

burned_counties_summary.notes: West Virginia's courthouse record losses stem from Civil War damage (Hampshire, Hardy, Jefferson) and post-war courthouse fires (Braxton 1897, Clay 1873, Logan 1904, Tucker 1872, Webster 1897). For Civil War-damaged counties in the Eastern Panhandle and Potomac Highlands, the Library of Virginia in Richmond often has pre-1863 copies that survived. For post-war fire counties, the WV Archives may have state copies of land grants and tax records. Census records, church records, and newspaper notices serve as substitute sources for burned county research.
